title: Rotational Motion
subject: Physics
content:
# Rotational Motion

## Quick Synopsis

Rotational motion mirrors linear motion: torque replaces force, angular momentum replaces momentum, and moment of inertia replaces mass. JEE often mixes rotation with translation and energy.

## Must Know

- Torque is the turning effect of force about an axis.
- Moment of inertia depends on mass distribution and axis.
- Rolling without slipping links translational and rotational variables.
- Angular momentum is conserved if net external torque about the chosen point is zero.
- Pure rotation uses rotational kinetic energy; rolling has both translational and rotational kinetic energy.
- Instantaneous centre of rotation helps solve rolling velocity problems.

## Formulas / Rules

- τ = r × F; |τ| = rF sinθ.
- τnet = Iα for fixed-axis rotation.
- L = Iω for rigid body about symmetry axis; Krot = 1/2 Iω².
- Rolling without slipping: vCM = Rω and aCM = Rα.
- Parallel axis theorem: I = ICM + Md²; perpendicular axis theorem for lamina: Iz = Ix + Iy.

## JEE Focus

- Pick torque axis to eliminate unknown forces.
- For rolling down incline, combine force equation, torque equation, and no-slip constraint.
- Use energy for rolling if friction is static and does no net work.
- Know standard moments of inertia for ring, disc, rod, sphere, and cylinder.

## Common Traps

- Static friction may provide torque without dissipating energy in pure rolling.
- Torque depends on chosen origin.
- Angular momentum can be conserved about one point but not another.
- Moment of inertia is not just mass; distribution matters.

## 10-Minute Revision Checklist

- Can you derive acceleration of rolling body on incline?
- Can you use angular momentum conservation?
- Can you apply parallel-axis theorem correctly?
- Can you locate instantaneous centre for rolling?
